frameworks.name: ComBat statistical harmonization
frameworks.one_line: Empirical-Bayes location/scale adjustment to remove site effects from neuroimaging features.

frameworks.name: Resting-state functional connectivity
frameworks.one_line: Pairwise BOLD correlation as the measurement target for harmonization.

frameworks.name: Graph-theoretic brain network analysis
frameworks.one_line: Network-level measures derived from connectivity matrices as harmonization targets.

core_question: Can a statistical harmonization technique remove residual multi-site scanner effects from resting-state fMRI functional connectivity and network measures?
key_assumptions: Phantom-based protocol homogenization leaves residual non-biological site variability
Site effects are separable from biological effects via location/scale modeling
Connectivity metric and brain parcellation choice modulate site-effect magnitude
key_findings: Site effects were detectable in connectivity and network measures despite phantom-homogenized acquisition
ComBat removed identified site effects across metrics and atlases
Post-harmonization increased statistical power to detect age associations
Optimal metric-atlas combinations yielded the largest power gains
interpretations: Statistical harmonization is necessary even after protocol homogenization in multi-site fMRI
ComBat generalizes from DTI/cortical thickness to functional connectivity features
